metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the definition of max_loc in the plan (e.g. 'max_loc = the highest single-file line count across all files in the scan root') so the executor does not need to infer it.
evidence: Description: 'populating AnalyzerResult.loc (per-file/per-language LOC + max)'
AC1: 'per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc'
No definition of max_loc's unit or scope anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: 'max_loc' is used in AC1 and the description without defining its semantics — it is ambiguous whether it means the maximum LOC across all files, per language, or a per-file field on the result.
findings.location: Description / AC1: 'per-file/per-language LOC + max'
scenarios: Executor implements max_loc as per-language max while consumer (story 3b30) expects a single file-level maximum, causing a silent contract mismatch.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Description or AC1: 'max_loc = scalar integer: the largest total-lines value across all scanned files (used as a complexity ceiling signal).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the return type/signature of `parse_scc_json`: state whether it returns a composite struct (e.g. `SccResult(files: list[dict], by_language: dict[str,int], max_loc: int)`) that both callers unpack, or two separate entry points, and update the grounding backend's usage note accordingly.
evidence: Current _scc_files (engine_b_backends.py:429-436) returns only flat file dicts; _metric_invoke only reads Code and Location fields from those dicts.
AC states: 'parse via the shared helper -> per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc' — requiring aggregation logic beyond what _scc_files currently does.
Plan says 'single normalizer' but does not specify what parse_scc_json returns or how the grounding backend (which only needs file-level iteration) will consume the richer struct.
findings.finding: The shared helper `parse_scc_json` is tasked with serving two callers with different data needs, but the plan never specifies its return type or signature. The existing `_scc_files` returns only flat file-dicts (used by the grounding backend to check `Code > threshold`). The new `scc_loc.py` also needs per-language aggregates and `max_loc` (per the AC). If `parse_scc_json` is a straight extraction of `_scc_files`, it won't natively produce per-language/max_loc — those aggregations would live only in `scc_loc.py`, violating the single-normalizer goal. The plan is silent on how the richer output needed by `scc_loc.py` is unified with the flat-file-dict output needed by the grounding backend.
findings.location: Design: expand-contract sequencing / AC: 'per-file/per-language LOC + max' / Verification: 'BOTH callers (single normalizer)'
scenarios: Implementer extracts _scc_files verbatim as parse_scc_json — grounding backend works, but scc_loc.py adds per-language/max_loc aggregation locally, silently re-splitting the normalizer.
Implementer adds aggregation inside parse_scc_json and returns a composite struct — grounding backend must be updated to unpack it; update is not mentioned in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Define `parse_scc_json(data: Any) -> SccResult` where `SccResult` holds `files` (flat list, for grounding threshold checks), `by_language` (lang→total-LOC dict, for AnalyzerResult), and `max_loc` (int). The grounding backend iterates `result.files`; `scc_loc.py` reads `result.by_language` and `result.max_loc`. This keeps all normalization in one place and makes the contract explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `parse_scc_json` must be designed to serve BOTH consumers: define its return type to include (a) per-file dicts for the grounding oversize filter AND (b) per-language LOC totals + max_loc for `scc_loc.py` — since `_scc_files` today only does (a), the extraction requires new aggregation logic that the plan must explicitly scope.
evidence: Plan: 'extract the existing scc JSON parse/normalize from engine_b_backends.py (_metric_invoke / _scc_files) into a shared helper (e.g. rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc.parse_scc_json)'
AC line 1: 'parses via the shared helper -> per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc'
Actual `_scc_files` at lines 429–436: iterates language entries, extracts file dicts — no aggregation, no max_loc
`_metric_invoke` (lines 367–426) adds only an oversize-threshold filter, not per-language totals or max computation
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `_scc_files` (and `_metric_invoke`) contain 'the existing scc JSON parse/normalize' logic sufficient to be extracted into `parse_scc_json` producing 'per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc'. But `_scc_files` (lines 429–436) only flattens per-file dicts from the language-grouped JSON array — it contains no per-language LOC aggregation and no max_loc computation. The extraction alone will not deliver the AC's promised outputs; new aggregation logic must also be written. The plan misframes this as a pure refactor/extract.
findings.location: Design section (step 1 / CONTRACT first) and AC line 1: 'per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc'
scenarios: Implementer extracts `_scc_files` verbatim and calls it `parse_scc_json`, ships `scc_loc.py`, then discovers per-language aggregation and max_loc are missing from the shared helper — forcing either new logic in the helper (breaking the 'no second normalizer' claim) or custom logic in `scc_loc.py` (violating the single-normalizer AC).

The grounding backend is pointed at `parse_scc_json` but the function signature changes to return aggregates instead of raw file dicts, silently breaking the oversize-threshold logic in `_metric_invoke`.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, replace 'extract the existing scc JSON parse/normalize' with 'create `parse_scc_json(data) -> SccResult` where `SccResult` holds both the flat file list (reused by the grounding oversize filter) and per-language aggregates + max_loc (new logic, consumed by scc_loc.py)'. Update the scope estimate to reflect the new aggregation code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete Python type/shape of `AnalyzerResult.loc` in ticket 3b30 (or in this plan's `_scc.py` spec) — specify key names (e.g. `per_file: dict[str,int]`, `per_language: dict[str,int]`, `max_loc: int`) so that `parse_scc_json`'s return type and `AnalyzerResult.loc` assignment are unambiguous for all callers.
evidence: Plan AC1: 'analyzers/scc_loc.py runs scc over the configured scan root, parses via the shared helper -> per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc'
Ticket 3b30 AC1: 'Analyzer protocol + AnalyzerResult (fields loc/complexity/duplication, each optional=None when unsupported)' — no concrete type for the loc field's internal structure
ABSENCE: neither the plan nor 3b30 specifies the Python type or key names for AnalyzerResult.loc
scc JSON has keys 'Code', 'Location', nested under language → Files array (confirmed in engine_b_backends.py:412-435), but the mapping to the loc field shape is never stated
findings.finding: The `AnalyzerResult.loc` field is referenced throughout this plan (AC1: 'per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc'; Description: 'populating AnalyzerResult.loc') but its concrete value vocabulary — the Python type, key names, and shape — is never defined in this plan or in its blocker 3b30, which only says 'fields loc/complexity/duplication, each optional=None when unsupported'. An executor implementing `parse_scc_json` cannot know whether `loc` is a `dict[str, int]` (filename→count), a `dict` with `{"per_file": ..., "per_language": ..., "max_loc": ...}`, a dataclass, or something else.
findings.location: AC1 ('per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc'); Description ('populating AnalyzerResult.loc'); blocker ticket 3b30 AnalyzerResult definition
scenarios: Executor of scc_loc.py chooses dict[str,int] for loc; executor of the consumer (0734) expects a dataclass with .max_loc attribute — silent AttributeError at runtime.
parse_scc_json returns flat list; AnalyzerResult.loc consumer expects per-language grouping — data is structurally correct but semantically wrong, no test catches it.
findings.suggested_fix: In 3b30's AnalyzerResult definition, concretize `loc` as e.g. `loc: LocResult | None` where `LocResult` is a small dataclass with `per_file: dict[str, int]`, `per_language: dict[str, int]`, and `max_loc: int`. Reference this type in the `parse_scc_json` return annotation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the new grounding → metrics/analyzers/_scc coupling direction in AGENTS.md or a brief ADR, and justify why the shared helper lives in metrics.analyzers rather than a neutral location (e.g., rebar.metrics.parsers or rebar.shared).
evidence: Plan states 'Point BOTH the grounding backend and the new analyzers/scc_loc.py at that shared helper' — establishing grounding as a consumer of metrics/analyzers/_scc.
No ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc update is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
The plan acknowledges the shared helper as the interface boundary but does not justify the coupling direction or record the decision.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new cross-component dependency (grounding/engine_b_backends.py importing from metrics/analyzers/_scc.py) without documenting the coupling direction in an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc. If the dependency arrow goes grounding → metrics-internals, this inverts or crosses a likely module-boundary convention and will silently accumulate as technical debt.
findings.location: Design: expand-contract sequencing / Files likely touched: src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py
scenarios: A future maintainer adds a grounding-layer abstraction and doesn't realize grounding already depends on a metrics.analyzers internal module.
The metrics package is restructured or versioned independently, breaking the grounding backend silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph note in AGENTS.md or a lightweight ADR explaining that `_scc.parse_scc_json` is intentionally shared between the grounding backend and the metrics analyzer, and that `rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc` is the canonical home for scc JSON normalization. Alternatively, place `_scc.py` in a neutral `rebar.metrics._parsers` or `rebar.shared` namespace to make the dependency direction obviously inward-only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `parse_scc_json(raw_json: str) -> list[dict]` accepts a raw JSON string (wrapping both `json.loads` and the `_scc_files` flattening logic), so there is truly a single normalizer for both the parse and struct-normalization steps.
evidence: Plan says: 'extract the existing scc JSON parse/normalize from `_metric_invoke` / `_scc_files` into a shared helper (e.g. `rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc.parse_scc_json`)'
In engine_b_backends.py line 397: `data = json.loads(res.stdout) ...` (inside `_metric_invoke`)
In engine_b_backends.py line 429: `def _scc_files(data: Any) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:` (takes pre-parsed dict, not raw JSON string)
findings.finding: The plan names `parse_scc_json` as the shared helper to extract, but the existing extractable unit in `engine_b_backends.py` is `_scc_files`, which normalizes *already-parsed* data structures — not raw JSON. The `json.loads` call lives separately in `_metric_invoke`. The plan says 'extract the existing scc JSON parse/normalize from `_metric_invoke` / `_scc_files`', implying `parse_scc_json` should accept a raw JSON string, but this boundary is unstated. If an implementer only renames `_scc_files` → `parse_scc_json` (without including the `json.loads` logic), the Verification AC ('`engine_b_backends` imports `parse_scc_json`') would pass superficially while `scc_loc.py` still duplicates the JSON parsing logic.
findings.location: Design: expand-contract sequencing, step 1 / Verification section
scenarios: Implementer moves only `_scc_files` → `parse_scc_json`; `json.loads` stays in `_metric_invoke`. `scc_loc.py` independently calls `json.loads` + calls `parse_scc_json`. The Verification import-assertion test passes but the 'single normalizer' guarantee is violated for the JSON-parse step.
findings.suggested_fix: Update step 1 of 'Design: expand-contract sequencing' to read: 'extract both the `json.loads` call and the `_scc_files` struct-normalization from `_metric_invoke` into `parse_scc_json(raw_json: str) -> list[dict[str, Any]]` in `rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc`; the function should raise/return `[]` on invalid JSON internally so callers never touch `json` directly.'

findings.finding: AC1's 'reuses the grounding scc seam' sub-claim has no concrete proving command: a unit test with a monkeypatched runner passes whether the executor shared the existing parser or wrote a fresh duplicate, so the structural requirement is unverifiable from the stated verification steps.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria, AC1: 'reuses the grounding scc seam'; Verification section
findings.suggested_fix: In the unit test file, add: `from rebar.grounding import engine_b_backends; assert hasattr(engine_b_backends, '<extracted_function_name>')` and import/call it inside `scc_loc.py`, making the dependency traceable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the scc JSON→AnalyzerResult.loc mapping rule with the AnalyzerResult schema (either in 3b30's type definition or in this plan's `_scc.py` spec section): explicitly state what `parse_scc_json(data) -> <type>` returns and how each scc JSON key ('Code', 'Location', language grouping) maps to the loc field.
evidence: Plan Design step 1: 'extract the existing scc JSON parse/normalize from engine_b_backends.py (_metric_invoke / _scc_files) into a shared helper (e.g. rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc.parse_scc_json)'
Plan AC3: 'The shared helper is used by BOTH callers (single normalizer)' — states reuse requirement but not what the normalizer must produce relative to the AnalyzerResult.loc schema
3b30 defines AnalyzerResult fields without specifying the internal structure of loc — the two specs never reference each other's type contract

Confirmed: existing _scc_files in engine_b_backends.py:429-436 only returns raw dicts with 'Code'/'Location' keys; it does not produce an AnalyzerResult-shaped output — so extraction alone is insufficient without an explicit mapping rule
findings.finding: The processing protocol that maps scc JSON keys ('Code', 'Location', the language→Files nesting) to the `AnalyzerResult.loc` structure is referenced implicitly by the `parse_scc_json` extraction directive ('extract the existing scc JSON parse/normalize') but is never co-located with — or cross-referenced from — the `AnalyzerResult` schema definition in 3b30. The two artefacts (schema in 3b30/analyzer.py and normalization rule in _scc.py) are fully decoupled in the plan's text, so an executor treating them as independent work items has no authoritative source for the mapping rule.
findings.location: Design section step 1 ('extract…into parse_scc_json'); AC3 ('single normalizer'); blocker 3b30 AnalyzerResult
scenarios: Executor extracts _scc_files as-is into parse_scc_json (returning list[dict]), then writes scc_loc.py to wrap it in an ad-hoc AnalyzerResult — the 'single normalizer' goal is met syntactically but the mapping lives in scc_loc.py, not the shared helper.
Two executors independently choose different field names for the loc mapping, breaking the integration at 0734.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command to Verification that asserts the output structure: the monkeypatched-runner unit test should assert on the returned AnalyzerResult.loc content (e.g. presence of per-file entries, per-language totals, and a max_loc value) not just that the call completes.
evidence: AC1: 'scc_loc.py runs scc over the configured scan root, parses via the shared helper -> per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc'
Verification: 'Unit with a monkeypatched runner seam (no scc needed in CI)' — no assertion on output content described
findings.finding: AC1 claims the analyzer produces 'per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc', but the Verification section describes only an import-assertion and a 'monkeypatched runner seam' test — neither is described as asserting on the actual output content (the AnalyzerResult.loc fields). The plan has no proving command that verifies the returned data structure matches the claimed output.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria AC1 / Verification section
scenarios: A test could pass (imports correct, no crash) while populating wrong fields or omitting max_loc entirely, and no described check would catch it.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Verification bullet: 'Unit with a monkeypatched runner seam asserts: (a) AnalyzerResult.loc contains a per-file mapping keyed by path, (b) a per-language summary, and (c) a max_loc integer > 0 for a non-empty fixture; absent-tool path returns Unavailable (not AnalyzerResult with zeros).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or supplement the grep/import assertion for `engine_b_backends` with a behavioral test: exercise the refactored backend with a mocked scc output and assert it produces the same normalized result as calling `parse_scc_json` directly (proving the shared helper is actually invoked and its result used correctly).
evidence: "Unit asserts the reuse by IMPORT: `from rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc import parse_scc_json` AND `engine_b_backends` imports the same symbol (grep/import assertion in the test)"

A grep for an import statement in engine_b_backends.py does not verify that the refactored caller produces correct normalized results; it verifies only textual presence of an import, which can pass while the actual behavior is broken (wrong arguments, dead import, result discarded).
findings.finding: The plan proposes verifying that `engine_b_backends.py` uses the shared `parse_scc_json` helper via a 'grep/import assertion in the test', which is a source-grep masquerading as a behavioral test. A grep (or even an import statement check) for the refactored caller passes even if the import is unused, if the call arguments are wrong, or if the refactored path doesn't actually route through the shared helper correctly — it does not verify that `engine_b_backends` produces correct normalized output after the refactor.
findings.location: Verification section: 'grep/import assertion in the test, proving scc_loc.py does not re-implement normalization' / tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py
scenarios: engine_b_backends.py is refactored to import parse_scc_json but calls it with wrong arguments or ignores its return value — grep/import assertion goes green, behavior is silently broken.
Import statement is present but guarded by a conditional that never executes in the test context — structural assertion passes, behavioral regression is missed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a unit test in `tests/unit/grounding/test_engine_b_backends.py` that monkeypatches the shell-out, feeds known scc JSON, and asserts the returned metric structure matches the output of `parse_scc_json(same_json)` — this proves end-to-end reuse behaviorally, not just structurally.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Verification step (or extend AC line 2) to assert that the `Unavailable` call sources `accruing_since` from a module-level `_ACCRUING_SINCE` constant, e.g. by asserting `scc_loc._ACCRUING_SINCE` exists and is the value passed to `Unavailable`.
evidence: 'Absent tool -> reuse Unavailable(reason, accruing_since); accruing_since from a module-level _ACCRUING_SINCE constant (matching the existing reader modules).'

AC line 2 only states 'Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)' without specifying that accruing_since must come from a _ACCRUING_SINCE constant — the AC would pass even if accruing_since were hardcoded inline.
No Verification step checks for the constant's existence or import.
findings.finding: The `_ACCRUING_SINCE` module-level constant is specified in Review-driven corrections ('accruing_since from a module-level _ACCRUING_SINCE constant matching the existing reader modules') but no Acceptance Criterion or Verification step covers its presence, value, or correct usage in the Unavailable call.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections (accruing_since requirement) vs Acceptance Criteria (AC line 2: 'Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)')
scenarios: Implementer passes a hardcoded datetime inline rather than a module-level constant, satisfying AC line 2 but violating the design constraint, and no test catches the difference.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC line 2 to read: 'scc absent / non-zero exit -> Unavailable(reason, accruing_since) where accruing_since is sourced from a module-level _ACCRUING_SINCE constant; test asserts scc_loc._ACCRUING_SINCE exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py` (new) to the 'Files likely touched' list.
evidence: Plan lists: 'src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/scc_loc.py (new), src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/_scc.py (new shared parse)' — no `__init__.py`
Existing `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` has an `__init__.py` (confirmed via directory listing)
Python requires `__init__.py` for a directory to be importable as a package (unless using namespace packages, which are not the project's pattern here)
findings.finding: The 'Files likely touched' list omits `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py`. Without this file the new `analyzers/` directory is not a Python package and `from rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc import parse_scc_json` (the import the Verification AC asserts) will raise `ModuleNotFoundError`.
findings.location: Files likely touched section
scenarios: CI runs `pytest tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py`; the `from rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc import parse_scc_json` import at the top fails with ModuleNotFoundError because `analyzers/` has no `__init__.py`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an entry: 'src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/__init__.py (new, empty or with package docstring)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and document in the plan) that the import-linter config permits `rebar.grounding` to import from `rebar.metrics.analyzers`, or identify an alternative placement for the shared helper that is importable from both packages without a layer violation.
evidence: Plan step 2: 'Point BOTH the grounding backend and the new analyzers/scc_loc.py at that shared helper'
ticket 9597 description: 'Respect the import-linter optionality ban-list: `import rebar` / `import rebar.metrics` must stay dependency-free'
No mention in the plan of checking/updating import-linter config for the new grounding→metrics edge
findings.finding: The refactor of `engine_b_backends.py` to `from rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc import parse_scc_json` introduces a new cross-package import: `rebar.grounding` → `rebar.metrics`. The project has import-linter rules enforced in CI (evidenced by `test_core_optionality.py` and ticket 9597 referencing 'import-linter/optionality check'). This new dependency edge is not mentioned in the plan, and if a rule bans `grounding` from importing `metrics` (or vice versa), the refactor will fail the import-linter check.
findings.location: Design: expand-contract sequencing, step 2 / Files likely touched (engine_b_backends.py)
scenarios: Implementer adds `from rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc import parse_scc_json` to `engine_b_backends.py`; import-linter fails CI with a layer violation if `grounding` is not allowed to import `metrics`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add `src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py` to the parent epic's (839b) file_impact and scope (update the parent first, re-review it), or (b) split the grounding refactor into a separate story so the leaf's scope is strictly confined to `src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/` and `_scc.py` — matching what the parent currently authorizes.
evidence: Leaf plan: 'extract the existing scc JSON parse/normalize from src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py ... Point BOTH the grounding backend and the new analyzers/scc_loc.py at that shared helper'
Leaf file_impact: 'src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py (point at shared helper)'
Parent epic 839b file_impact lists: analyzer.py, analyzers/, git_metrics.py, pyproject.toml, _config_schema.py, docs/config.md — no mention of grounding/engine_b_backends.py
Parent epic 839b description and ACs are silent on the grounding module entirely
findings.finding: The leaf plans to modify src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py (re-pointing it at the new shared _scc.py helper), but the parent epic (839b) does not include the grounding module in its scope, plan, or file_impact list — making this an out-of-scope addition relative to the parent's contract.
findings.location: Design: expand-contract sequencing, step 1–2; Files likely touched (engine_b_backends.py entry)
scenarios: A reviewer approving the leaf's changeset would see a modification to the grounding subsystem that the epic's plan never authorized — raising unexpected scope, risk to grounding module stability, and a plan-attestation mismatch.
If the grounding refactor causes a regression in engine_b_backends.py, it cannot be traced back to any parent-authorized work item.
findings.suggested_fix: If the reuse/deduplication goal is important (and it is a good engineering choice), add a brief note to the parent epic's file_impact and design acknowledging that engine_b_backends.py will be re-pointed at the shared _scc.py helper. That single-line update to 839b brings the leaf back into subset alignment without requiring a new story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 3b30/9597/e7a0 are true prerequisites (depends_on = must complete first) or truly concurrent siblings — and if 3b30 defines the AnalyzerResult interface scc_loc.py implements, mark it as a hard prerequisite, not a sibling.
evidence: 'Deps are SIBLING stories: 3b30 (interface), 9597 (extra), e7a0 (config scan roots).'
'Dependencies: 3b30, 9597, e7a0 (recorded depends_on edges to all three).'
If 3b30 defines the interface scc_loc.py must implement, proceeding in parallel risks implementing against a moving/undefined interface.
findings.finding: The Dependencies section describes 3b30/9597/e7a0 as 'SIBLING stories' (Review-driven corrections) with 'depends_on edges to all three' (Dependencies section), which is contradictory: sibling stories are concurrent/parallel, but depends_on edges imply sequential prerequisites. An implementer reading this cannot determine whether to wait for 3b30's interface definition before implementing scc_loc.py or proceed in parallel.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections ('Deps are SIBLING stories: 3b30, 9597, e7a0') vs Dependencies section ('depends_on edges to all three')
scenarios: Implementer treats all three as true prerequisites and blocks; or treats them as parallel and implements scc_loc.py against an interface that 3b30 hasn't finalized yet, requiring rework.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'SIBLING stories' to 'prerequisite stories' for 3b30 (since scc_loc.py must implement the interface it defines), and reserve 'sibling' only for 9597/e7a0 if they truly are concurrent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and implement a subprocess timeout for the `scc` shell-out (e.g., `subprocess.run(..., timeout=60)`) and map `TimeoutExpired` to `Unavailable(reason='scc timed out', accruing_since=_ACCRUING_SINCE)` in `scc_loc.py`.
evidence: AC states 'runs scc over the configured scan root' with no mention of timeout.
The failure handling only covers 'absent / non-zero exit' — a hung process is neither.
No timeout, deadline, or circuit-breaker is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a timeout for the `scc` subprocess invocation. On a large repository or a system where scc is installed but misbehaves, the shell-out can block indefinitely, hanging the calling thread/process with no way to recover.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria / analyzers/scc_loc.py (new)
scenarios: scc is installed but encounters a filesystem hang (NFS, FUSE) on the scan root.
scc is installed on a very large monorepo and takes many minutes; the metrics pipeline stalls waiting.
An I/O-saturated CI host causes scc to block on directory traversal.
findings.suggested_fix: In the runner seam inside `scc_loc.py`, use `subprocess.run([...], timeout=_SCC_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, ...)` where `_SCC_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` is a module-level constant (e.g., 120). Catch `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` in the same error-handling block as non-zero exit and convert it to `Unavailable`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `logger.warning(...)` (or equivalent structured log) in the Unavailable path of `scc_loc.py` so that scc absence, non-zero exit, and timeout are each observable in application logs without requiring a debugger.
evidence: Plan only specifies Unavailable return value for failures — no logging, no metric counter, no trace event.
Observability section is entirely absent from the plan.
The existing reader modules' pattern (accruing_since) is reused, but no statement that those modules also log/meter is made or referenced.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify any logging or metric instrumentation for the scc failure path (absent tool, non-zero exit, or future timeout). The `Unavailable` return value surfaces the error to the caller but leaves operators with no observable signal in logs/metrics when the analyzer silently degrades.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — 'scc absent / non-zero exit -> Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)'
scenarios: scc is silently missing in a production environment; the metrics dashboard shows stale/unavailable LOC data with no alert and no log line pointing to the cause.
An operator must bisect why LOC metrics disappeared after a dependency upgrade, with no log trail.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (in the plan or a comment) that the 2-call-site `_scc.parse_scc_json` abstraction is justified by a maintenance/correctness rationale (single normalizer, no duplication) rather than raw reuse count, to satisfy the YAGNI sub-check.
evidence: "CONTRACT first: extract the existing scc JSON parse/normalize … into a shared helper (e.g. rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc.parse_scc_json)"
"Point BOTH the grounding backend and the new analyzers/scc_loc.py at that shared helper — no second JSON normalizer"
Only 2 named call-sites in the entire plan for `parse_scc_json`
findings.finding: The new `_scc.parse_scc_json` abstraction is shared by exactly 2 call-sites (the grounding backend and `scc_loc.py`), which is below the plan-rubric's ≥3 call-site threshold for justifying a new shared abstraction. The plan's stated rationale (avoid a second JSON normalizer) is a valid maintenance argument, but by the YAGNI sub-check the abstraction is not yet justified by call-site count alone.
findings.location: Design: expand-contract sequencing / Files likely touched: src/rebar/metrics/analyzers/_scc.py
scenarios: If a future LOC adapter is never added, `_scc.py` remains a 2-call-site module; the abstraction adds a layer of indirection without meeting the ≥3 threshold.
If the grounding backend's scc usage evolves separately, keeping them coupled through a shared helper may require extra coordination.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line rationale note in the Design section, e.g. "_scc.py is justified as a single normalizer (correctness invariant), not purely by call-site count; a third caller is anticipated with story 9597."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm a third call-site is in scope (or already exists) before extracting `_scc.parse_scc_json`; if only 2 callers are planned, consider inlining a thin `_parse_scc_json` private helper directly in `scc_loc.py` and accepting a one-line duplication of the list-flatten, deferring extraction until a third consumer appears.
evidence: grep hit: `src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py:412: for entry in _scc_files(data):` — sole existing call-site
grep hit: `src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py:429: def _scc_files(data: Any)` — 7-line function, trivial list-flatten
`parse_scc_json` returns 0 grep hits — does not yet exist anywhere in codebase
Sibling tickets 3b30 (Analyzer protocol), 9597 (extra), e7a0 (config scan roots) name no third scc JSON consumer
Plan phrase: 'Point BOTH the grounding backend and the new analyzers/scc_loc.py at that shared helper' — explicitly only 2 callers
findings.finding: The proposed `rebar.metrics.analyzers._scc.parse_scc_json` abstraction has only 2 confirmed call-sites (the existing grounding backend + the new `scc_loc.py`), which is below the Rule-of-Three threshold (≥3) for a justified extraction. `_scc_files` is a 7-line list-flattener with one existing call-site in `engine_b_backends.py`; no third caller is in scope across the named sibling tickets (3b30, 9597, e7a0).
findings.location: Design: expand-contract sequencing / AC: 'The shared helper is used by BOTH callers (single normalizer)'
scenarios: If no third caller materialises, `_scc.py` becomes a single-purpose wrapper adding indirection without reducing duplication.
The cross-subsystem coupling (grounding imports from metrics/analyzers/) may surprise future maintainers without clear ownership of the shared module.
findings.suggested_fix: Keep `_scc_files` in `engine_b_backends.py` (its natural home). In `scc_loc.py`, write a local `_parse_scc_json` that duplicates the 7-line flatten. Link the two with a comment ('# same shape as engine_b_backends._scc_files — extract to shared helper when a third caller appears'). This satisfies AC 'no second JSON normalizer' at the cost of one deliberate, documented copy, avoids the premature cross-subsystem dependency, and keeps the Rule-of-Three gate explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that 3b30 is recorded as a hard `depends_on` blocker (not just a peer sibling) so that `scc_loc.py` cannot be started before `AnalyzerResult` exists in `src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py`.
evidence: Plan description: 'populating `AnalyzerResult.loc` (per-file/per-language LOC + max_loc)'
Grep for `AnalyzerResult` across all of `src/rebar/`: no matches found
Ticket 3b30 status: 'open'; its signed manifest: 'dep absent src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py'
Ticket 3b30 is listed as a dependency ('Deps are SIBLING stories: 3b30 (interface)') but the plan does not make this ticket strictly blocked by 3b30 landing first
findings.finding: The plan references `AnalyzerResult.loc` as a settled contract the new `scc_loc.py` will populate, but `AnalyzerResult` does not yet exist in the codebase — it is defined by sibling story 3b30, which is still 'open' and whose own signed manifest records `dep absent src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py`. If this ticket is implemented before 3b30 merges, there is no `AnalyzerResult` to populate.
findings.location: Description ('populating AnalyzerResult.loc') and AC line 1
scenarios: An implementer picks up this ticket while 3b30 is still open, tries to import `AnalyzerResult` from `rebar.metrics.analyzer`, gets an ImportError, and either invents a local definition (defeating the interface-unification goal) or is blocked with no clear guidance.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'depends_on: 3b30' as a blocking dependency edge (matching the same `depends_on` relation used between 9597→3b30 and e7a0→3b30), and add a note: 'AnalyzerResult is not yet present; this ticket cannot begin until 3b30 merges'.

findings.finding: The described work of extracting/refactoring the scc parse logic from `engine_b_backends.py` into a shared seam is not covered by any acceptance criterion, making it unverifiable and an orphan deliverable.
findings.location: Files likely touched: src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py (extract shared scc parse) / Acceptance criteria (all three ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'The scc JSON-parse helper is extracted from `engine_b_backends.py` into a shared function/module; existing unit tests for the grounding engine continue to pass without modification.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'grounding `measure_tree`/`_metric_invoke` precedent returns None/abstains, so map that to Unavailable here,' implying both return a None-like value that maps to Unavailable. However, `_metric_invoke` does NOT return None — it returns a `list[dict]` of `ev.abstain()` records (verified at lines 376–426 of engine_b_backends.py). Only `measure_tree` returns Python None. An implementer extracting the shared scc seam will find a list-of-dicts interface, not a None, making the 'map that to Unavailable' guidance misleading about the actual extraction contract.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections — 'note the grounding `measure_tree`/`_metric_invoke` precedent returns None/abstains, so map that to Unavailable here'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Review-driven corrections, replace 'the grounding `measure_tree`/`_metric_invoke` precedent returns None/abstains' with: 'the extractable seam is the JSON-parse + `_scc_files` normalization inside `_metric_invoke` (lines 396–436); `_metric_invoke` itself returns `list[dict]` of `ev.abstain()` records on failure — the new `scc_loc.py` wraps the extracted parse result directly into `Unavailable` as a new adaptation layer.'

findings.finding: The plan's 'extract/share' of `_scc_files` from `engine_b_backends.py` results in only 2 call-sites (the existing metric backend + the new `scc_loc.py`) and crosses a subsystem boundary (`grounding` → `metrics`). Importing a private `_`-prefixed function from `grounding` into `metrics/analyzers/` couples two independent subsystems for a helper whose body is ~8 lines. The plan does not specify a neutral extraction point (e.g., a shared utility module), leaving implementors to either violate encapsulation or silently inline — both without guidance.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections: 'REUSE the existing scc `--format json` shell-out + parser: `src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py` (`_metric_invoke` / `_scc_files`)' / Files likely touched: `src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py (extract shared scc parse)`
findings.suggested_fix: Since the function is only ~8 lines and there are exactly 2 callers across a subsystem boundary, the least-coupling option is to inline an equivalent helper directly in `scc_loc.py` and leave `_scc_files` in `engine_b_backends.py` untouched. If sharing is truly desired, extract to `src/rebar/grounding/_scc_parse.py` and import from there in both places — but document the cross-subsystem dependency explicitly.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
